concepts.title: Root Mean Square (RMS) Value of Alternating Current
concepts.description: The RMS value of an alternating current is the square root of the average value of the square of the instantaneous values. It is also known as the effective value of the current. It is denoted by I rms.

concepts.title: Alternating Current and Direct Current
concepts.description: Alternating current (AC) is a type of electric current that periodically reverses direction, whereas direct current (DC) flows in one direction only.

concepts.title: Peak Value of Alternating Current
concepts.description: The peak value of alternating current is the maximum value of the instantaneous current, which is I 0 = I rms × √2.

concepts.title: RMS Value Formula
concepts.description: To remember the RMS value formula, use the mnemonic: 'RMS = I 0 / √2'.

concepts.title: Confusing RMS and Peak Value
concepts.description: Students often confuse the RMS value and peak value of alternating current. Remember that RMS value is the effective value, while peak value is the maximum value.

concepts.title: Advantages of Alternating Current
concepts.description: Alternating current has several advantages over direct current, including easier transformation of voltage, reduced energy loss, and increased efficiency.

concepts.title: Unit of RMS Value
concepts.description: The unit of RMS value is the same as the unit of current, which is amperes (A).

concepts.title: Power in Alternating Current
concepts.description: The power in an alternating current circuit is given by the formula P = I rms × V rms, where V rms is the root mean square value of the voltage.
